nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, shaded, slow-flowing lowland rainforest streams below 1000 m elevation with clear or slightly tannin-stained water, silty or sandy substrate, heavy leaf litter accumulation, and dense marginal vegetation; low conductivity blackwater conditions

decorPreferences: dense marginal planting (Cryptocoryne, Java fern, mosses)
leaf litter bed (Indian almond / catappa leaves)
driftwood and twisted roots for cover
dark substrate (fine sand or fine gravel)
open mid-water swimming space for schooling

diet.notes: Micropredatory in the wild, consuming small invertebrates, terrestrial insects (Diptera, Coleoptera), and organic detritus. In the aquarium, provide regular small meals of live or frozen micro-foods — Daphnia, Artemia nauplii, bloodworm, and micro-worms — supplemented with high-quality small-particle dried flakes and granules. Varied diet supports optimal coloration. Mouth is small; avoid large-particle foods.

goodWith: dwarf shrimp (with caution — fry may be predated)
avoidWith: larger or boisterous fish that cause stress
fast, competitive feeders that outcompete for food

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species summary image; confirm availability and license before use.

summary: A Sri Lankan endemic soft-water schooling micro-fish reaching only 4 cm, the Pearly Rasbora (Rasboroides vaterifloris) belongs to the separate genus Rasboroides and is not a true Rasbora despite the name. IUCN-listed as Endangered, it thrives in dim, leaf-littered blackwater biotopes and rewards patient keepers with brilliant orange-red coloration.
careNotes:
The Pearly Rasbora is a delicate, specialist fish suited to experienced aquarists who can replicate its soft, acidic blackwater habitat. Despite the common name, Rasboroides vaterifloris belongs to the distinct genus Rasboroides within family Danionidae and is not a true Rasbora — a taxonomic point worth noting when sourcing livestock. The species is endemic to the wet-zone river systems of southwestern Sri Lanka (principally the Kalu basin following 2013 range revision), where it inhabits cool, heavily shaded lowland rainforest streams with very low conductivity, tannin-stained water, and deep leaf litter.

Water chemistry is critical: maintain pH 5.5–7.0 with soft water (gH 1–8 dGH) and temperature 23–27°C. Hard or alkaline water leads to rapid decline. Use reverse-osmosis or very soft tap water; supplement with dried Indian almond (catappa) leaves or commercially prepared blackwater extract to lower pH and add tannins naturally. Avoid bright lighting — a heavily planted, shaded setup with driftwood and leaf litter best replicates the natural habitat and reduces chronic stress.

Keep in groups of at least 8 (ideally 10+): isolated individuals or small groups remain permanently stressed and rarely display full coloration. The species is retiring and can be outcompeted easily; house only with equally small, non-boisterous companions such as Boraras species, Trigonostigma species, dwarf loaches, and Otocinclus. Do not introduce to a biologically immature (uncycled) aquarium — sensitivity to ammonia and nitrite spikes is high. Gentle filtration with a flow rate of 4–5× tank volume is appropriate; avoid strong currents.

Specimens often arrive in poor condition after shipping stress. Quarantine is strongly recommended. Wild-caught fish are increasingly scarce and highly colored individuals are rare; captive-bred stock should be preferred wherever available. The IUCN classifies this species as Endangered (EN) due to habitat loss from deforestation and selective collection pressure on the most vivid wild individuals.
breedingNotes: Egg-scattering spawner with no parental care. Condition a group of 5–6 pairs on live foods (Daphnia, Artemia, micro-worms) for 1–2 weeks, then transfer to a dedicated soft-water breeding tank (pH 6.0–6.5, temperature ~25°C, very soft water, fine-leaved plants or java moss as spawning substrate). Pairs spawn over 4–5 days; remove adults promptly as eggs and fry will be consumed. Eggs hatch in 24–48 hours; fry become free-swimming after 3–5 days. Start fry on infusoria or Paramecium cultures, progressing to Artemia nauplii and micro-worms as size increases. Growth is slow; sexual maturity takes several months. Fry are sensitive to water quality — perform frequent small water changes using pre-conditioned soft water.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Rasboroides vaterifloris
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided temperature (23–27°C), pH (5.5–7.0), hardness (18–143 ppm ≈ 1–8 dGH), minimum tank dimensions (60×30 cm, ~54 L), group size (6–10 minimum), diet (micropredator; live/frozen plus dried), breeding methodology (egg scatterer, 5–6 pairs, 24–48 h hatch, fry feeding progression), native range (Kalu, Bentota, Gin, Nilwala basins, SW Sri Lanka), habitat (shaded lowland forest streams, leaf litter, low conductivity), compatible tankmates, common names, and care warnings regarding water sensitivity and import stress.

additional.name: FishBase — Rasboroides vaterifloris
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (4.0 cm TL), temperature (25–29°C), pH (~6.0), hardness (5–8 dH), taxonomy (Danionidae, Cypriniformes, subfamily Rasborinae), native range (Kalu basin, Sri Lanka), habitat (shallow cool shaded forest streams, silty substrate, leaf debris), diet (terrestrial insects, detritus), IUCN status (Endangered EN, assessed August 2019, criteria B1ab(iii)+2ab(iii)).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Rasboroides vaterifloris
additional.notes: Confirmed IUCN Endangered status (2020 assessment), endemic range restricted to Kalu basin per 2013 phylogeographic revision, synonymy of R. nigromarginatus with R. vaterifloris established 2018, max size 4 cm TL, common names (pearly rasbora, fire rasbora, vateria flower rasbora), present in aquarium trade.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scattering spawner requiring soft acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5), conditioned breeding group of 5–6 pairs, and fine-leaved plant or moss spawning substrate. Eggs hatch 24–48 h; fry free-swimming after 3–5 days. Fry rearing is slow and water-quality sensitive — start on Paramecium/infusoria, progress to Artemia nauplii. Remove adults immediately after spawning period.
